reasoning: The base rate for total Russian gas imports into the EU in 2024 was around 45-52 bcm for the entire year, which averages to about 11.2-13 bcm per quarter. To hit 15 bcm in a single quarter of 2025, imports would have to surge to a 60 bcm/year annualized rate. However, the background structurally prevents this: Russia ended pipeline deliveries through Ukraine in January 2025, which permanently removes roughly 14-15 bcm/year (or ~3.5 bcm/quarter) of physical delivery capacity to the EU. The only remaining major import routes for Russian gas to the EU are the TurkStream pipeline (which has a maximum European capacity of around 15.75 bcm/year, or ~4 bcm/quarter) and LNG (which historically supplies roughly 18-20 bcm/year to the EU, or ~5 bcm/quarter). Combined, maximum remaining practical capacity is roughly 9-10 bcm/quarter. Given the EU's broader commitment to phase out Russian fossil fuels via REPowerEU and lack of physical pipeline infrastructure to replace the Ukrainian route, it is physically and politically near-impossible for imports to reach 15 bcm in any quarter in 2025. The crowd consensus at 0.01 confirms this as a near-certain NO. I am providing the lowest allowed forecast of 0.02.
